packageReport.prompt: electric creature-battle title letters with arcade speed, comet-fang weirdness, chunky sticker energy, sharp readable monster-showdown drama Codex learning constraints: Fused-engine diagnosis: clean counters helped, but generic athletic chamfers are not enough. Add oversized creature-specific events: jaw apertures, fang terminals, broken shoulders, hooked counters, and voltage bridges that remain visible at mobile proof size. Prompt patches: Next pass: judge only the undecorated black proof first. Keep the reverse-slanted monster-title stance, but replace repeated esports chamfer chips with one oversized electric-fang structural event per uppercase and numeral. Make C an open jaw with a clear lower fang, G a jaw with an unmistakable inward voltage hook, O a closed charged ring with one large asymmetric break, 0 a slashed form clearly unlike O, 6 an open top hook with lower bite, 8 two pinched counters, and 9 a top loop with a descending fang tail. Apply mint fill, heavy black sticker outline, and purple shadow only after the raw silhouettes pass. Mobile hero: larger centered two-line VOLTAGE / FANG with no crop and less dead space. Next pass: judge only the undecorated black proof first. Keep the reverse-slanted monster-title stance, but remove repeated small chips as the main texture. Each uppercase and numeral needs one large structural electric-fang event visible on mobile. Prioritize C=open jaw with lower fang, G=jaw plus oversized inward voltage hook, O=closed ring with one major voltage break, 0=clear slash unlike O, 6=open top hook plus lower bite, 8=two visibly pinched counters, 9=top loop with descending fang tail. Apply mint fill, black sticker outline, and purple shadow only after the raw silhouettes pass. Lessons: Do not approve Voltage Fang when the decorated hero carries more concept than the raw alphabet. Approval requires mobile-visible, individualized electric-fang silhouettes, especially in C/G/O and 0/6/8/9, plus deliberate mobile scale and tighter proof spacing. Do not approve Voltage Fang when the decorated hero carries the concept better than the raw alphabet. Approval requires visibly individualized electric-fang silhouettes in the black proof, especially C/G/O and 0/6/8/9. Do not approve Voltage Fang while the decorated hero is stronger than the raw alphabet or while mobile crops the title. Approval requires complete mobile composi...

limitations: Straight-line contours only; no cubic/quadratic curve recovery from QBT polygons.
No OpenType layout features are generated by this script.
Variable fonts require UFO/DesignSpace production source, not this static build.

brief.concept: A high-energy display-font package generated from a prompt, with procedural QBT source plus an optional curated open-font remix lane for production-quality static TTF/WOFF2 output.
tasteDecisions: Weight is intentionally loud at 125.0 units, so the face reads as title/display rather than body text.
Slant is set to -16.1 degrees for forward motion.
Spike energy 0.29 and jitter 0.02 create controlled weirdness while preserving alphabet recognition.
Spacing is conservative before kerning; this generator prioritizes usable proofs over tight logo-only spectacle.
The workflow avoids arbitrary font scraping and records prompt, seed, source font, license, and deterministic transforms for provenance.
Every uppercase and numeral has a recorded construction idea; Codex should reject the result if those ideas are not visible in the raw black proof.
The fused-outline engine uses single silhouette contours and reversed counter contours before decorative color treatment.

glyphConstruction.0: slashed zero with a different counter rhythm than O
glyphConstruction.1: offset flag and grounded base notch
glyphConstruction.2: top bite that turns into a fast diagonal ramp
glyphConstruction.3: stacked jaws with a hard center shelf
glyphConstruction.4: open triangular frame with a sharp cross-stab
glyphConstruction.5: clipped top shelf and lower bite
glyphConstruction.6: open top hook with a distinct lower loop bite
glyphConstruction.7: long falling lightning slash with one midair nick
glyphConstruction.8: two counters pinched by an offset center voltage seam
glyphConstruction.9: top loop and descending fang tail
glyphConstruction.A: split peak fang with a dropped voltage crossbar
glyphConstruction.B: two separate bowl bites so the upper and lower lobes do different jobs
glyphConstruction.C: open creature jaw with a lower fang spur
glyphConstruction.D: heavy back stem with one dorsal lightning bite
glyphConstruction.E: staggered electric arms with a broken middle shoulder
glyphConstruction.F: top-heavy arcade flag with a missing lower tooth
glyphConstruction.G: C jaw plus an inward voltage hook
glyphConstruction.H: paired pillars joined by a cracked lightning bridge
glyphConstruction.I: narrow charged spine with oversized caps
glyphConstruction.J: hooked tail that turns into a fang
glyphConstruction.K: broken knee where the diagonals collide
glyphConstruction.L: grounded foot with a forward bite
glyphConstruction.N: single bolt diagonal cutting across two blunt stems
glyphConstruction.O: closed charged ring with one major asymmetric voltage break
glyphConstruction.P: upper bowl punch with a quiet lower stem
glyphConstruction.Q: O ring plus a long comet tail
glyphConstruction.R: punched bowl and aggressive kicked leg
glyphConstruction.S: serpentine center with two opposing speed cuts
glyphConstruction.T: oversized top rail and narrow charged stem
glyphConstruction.U: bucket bottom with a bitten right shoulder
glyphConstruction.W: double fang rhythm with uneven outer claws
glyphConstruction.X: crossing voltage shards with a central collision chip
glyphConstruction.Y: forked upper arms feeding one blunt stem
glyphConstruction.Z: lightning rail with a snapped lower exit

modernSupport.variablePreflight: Light/Bold compatible QBT masters and interpolation proofs can be generated.

proofStrings:
VOLTAGE FANG
THE QUICK
BROWN TOOL
FANGS
VOLTAGE
& ODD
GLYPHS!
HAMBURGE
FONTSIV
0123456789

critique.memoryLesson: Do not approve Voltage Fang while the mobile raw proof still relies on small internal nicks and repeated clipped-corner chamfers. Approval requires C/G/O/Q and 0/6/8/9 to separate through large exterior silhouette decisions that remain obvious at mobile size.
nextEdits: Redraw C with a single oversized lower jaw fang that changes the outer silhouette, visible in the first mobile row.
Make G's voltage hook a thick inward bar that visibly intrudes from the right side and changes the white counter shape.
Keep O closed, but give it one large asymmetric exterior charge break rather than multiple small clipped-corner cues.
Extend Q into a clearer comet tail while increasing right sidebearing or tail angle so QUICK does not jam.
Separate 0/6/8/9 by primary structure: 0 as slashed ring, 6 as open-top hook/lower loop, 8 as two stacked counters, 9 as loop plus descending fang.
Calm support glyph chamfers so the anchors become the style carriers instead of every letter shouting the same trick.
critique.promptPatch: Next pass: keep the raw black reverse-slanted monster-title weight, but redraw only C/G/O/Q and 0/6/8/9 first. C needs one oversized lower exterior jaw fang visible in the mobile proof. G needs a bold inward voltage hook/bar that changes the silhouette and counter, not a small nick. O must stay closed with one major asymmetric exterior charge break. Q must be closed O plus a longer comet tail with enough spacing after it. Separate 0/6/8/9 by counter architecture, not repeated clipped-corner texture. Reduce generic esports chamfers across support letters.
critique.referenceStrategy: Use mobile black proof as the approval gate. Compare C/G/O/Q and 0/6/8/9 as isolated glyphs, in VOLTAGE FANG, THE QUICK, and the numeral row before adding color, outline, shadow, or hero treatment. Do not judge from the desktop specimen alone.
strongestDecisions: Heavy italic display mass gives VOLTAGE FANG and THE QUICK immediate arcade-title energy.
O stays closed in words and remains more readable than prior open-ring risk.
The 8 and 9 are among the better numerals: their counter structures separate reasonably well at mobile size.
Support alphabet is broadly readable, with A, E, X, V, T, K, M, N, W, and Z carrying the speed style without collapsing.
critique.verdict: Reject for another anchor pass. The face has usable monster-title weight, speed, and a coherent reverse-slanted stance, but the raw proof still depends too much on repeated clipped-corner esports construction. The key C/G/O/Q and 0/6/8/9 anchors are improved but not yet strong enough as mobile-visible exterior silhouette events.
visualRisks: At mobile size, the anchor details shrink into small black nicks instead of carrying the identity.
Repeated diagonal bites make letters feel interchangeable, especially C/G/O/Q and 0/6/8/9.
The reverse slant and tight heavy forms can create uneven word texture in longer lines.
Some counters are close to filling in, especially in G, Q, 6, and smaller proof rows.
weakestProblems: C does not yet have one unmistakable lower jaw fang at mobile size; it reads mostly as a clipped angular C.
G uses a small internal hook/bar rather than a bold silhouette-changing voltage event.
Q tail is present but not a long comet gesture; in text it reads like a modest spur and can crowd the following rhythm.
Generic clipped-corner chamfers remain the dominant texture across too many glyphs, making the concept feel derivative of esports/block gamer alphabets.
0, 6, and O still share too much construction language; their differences rely on inner cuts more than exterior silhouette.

critique.memoryLesson: Do not approve Voltage Fang while C/G/O/Q still separate mainly through small internal nicks or repeated clipped-corner chamfers. Approval requires large exterior anchor silhouettes that remain obvious in the raw black mobile proof.
nextEdits: Redraw C with one large lower exterior fang that changes the outer contour, not just the counter edge.
Redraw G from C but add a bold inward hook/bar that visibly cuts into the counter and changes the right-side silhouette.
Make O a calmer closed ring with one oversized asymmetric outer charge break so it reads as its own glyph, not a clipped C-like shape.
Keep Q closed and extend the comet tail, but add more right sidebearing so following letters do not crowd it.
Rebuild 0/6/8/9 from separate counter plans: slashed oval zero, open-top six, simpler stacked eight, and top-loop nine with a longer descending fang.
Reduce repeated clipped-corner bevels on support letters so the anchors carry the identity.
critique.promptPatch: Next pass: keep the raw black reverse-slanted monster-title weight, but redraw C/G/O/Q and 0/6/8/9 as large silhouette-first anchors. C needs one huge lower exterior jaw fang visible in the mobile proof. G needs a thick inward voltage hook/bar that changes the counter and right outline. O must stay closed with one oversized asymmetric exterior charge break. Q must be closed O plus a longer comet tail with protected spacing after it. Separate 0/6/8/9 by counter architecture, not clipped-corner texture. Remove most generic esports chamfers from support letters.
critique.referenceStrategy: Use the current A/E/X/T/V/W/Y/Z as acceptable support-energy references, but treat C/G/O/Q and 0/6/8/9 as failed anchor references. Compare the next raw mobile proof first at specimen size before judging any decorated hero treatment.
strongestDecisions: Strong black mass, forward slant, and angular rhythm give the specimen immediate arcade-creature title energy.
Word proofs are mostly readable, especially VOLTAGE FANG, THE QUICK, and BROWN TOOL.
Q has the clearest concept move among the anchors because the comet tail is visible in both desktop and mobile proofs.
A, E, X, T, V, W, Y, and Z support the style with sharp, fast silhouettes without becoming unreadable.
critique.verdict: Energetic and usable as a raw display proof, but not approved. The reverse-slanted monster-title stance is working, yet the promised anchor-glyph reset is only partially visible. C/G/O/Q and 0/6/8/9 still depend too much on repeated clipped-corner construction and small internal cuts instead of large mobile-visible silhouette decisions.
visualRisks: At mobile size, C/G/O/Q differentiation remains fragile in dense words.
Small white bites and center nicks may fill in or vanish at small sizes, low resolution, or vinyl/print production.
Q tails can collide visually in tighter setting unless spacing after Q is deliberately protected.
The repeated slant and chamfer formula may make words feel monotonous over longer headings.
weakestProblems: C still lacks an unmistakable oversized lower exterior jaw fang at mobile size.
G is too close to C; the inward voltage hook reads as a small internal detail, not a structural silhouette event.
O is closed, but its asymmetric charge break is not major enough to distinguish it strongly from the broader clipped-corner system.
0, 6, 8, and 9 separate better than the letters, but their shared chamfer language still dominates over distinct counter architecture.
The face still carries a generic esports bevel texture across many support glyphs, weakening the originality of the monster-fang idea.

critique.memoryLesson: Do not approve Voltage Fang while C/G/O/Q remain differentiated mainly by small interior cuts and repeated octagonal chamfers. Approval requires mobile-visible exterior silhouette anchors, especially a true C jaw fang and a true G voltage hook.
nextEdits: Redraw C with one oversized lower-right exterior fang that changes the whole footprint, not a small inner tooth.
Redraw G with a thick inward bar/hook that visibly changes both the white counter and the right-side black outline.
Make O a closed ring with one unmistakable exterior charge break or swelling asymmetry, while keeping it clearly closed.
Keep Q as O plus tail, but lengthen the comet tail and reserve right-side spacing so it survives word settings.
Separate 6 from G by giving 6 a clear lower-loop counter and a different top entry, not the same C shell.
Reduce repeated chamfers on non-anchor caps after the anchors work; let the anchor silhouettes set the style vocabulary.
critique.promptPatch: Next pass: reject small-nick differentiation. Redraw C/G/O/Q and 0/6/8/9 as silhouette thumbnails before expanding the alphabet. C must have one huge lower exterior jaw fang visible in the mobile raw proof. G must have a thick inward voltage hook/bar that changes the counter and the right outline. O must remain closed but gain one major asymmetric exterior charge event. Q must be closed O plus a longer comet tail with protected spacing. Separate 6 from G and 0 from O through counter architecture, not clipped-corner texture. Calm the support alphabet by removing generic esports bevel repetition.
critique.referenceStrategy: Use the current proof only for weight, slant, and black-mass attitude. Build a new anchor sheet at mobile size first: C/G/O/Q and 0/6/8/9 in repeated rows, then approve only if each glyph is identifiable by exterior silhouette before internal cuts are considered.
strongestDecisions: Reverse slant, heavy weight, and polygonal cuts give the specimen immediate arcade-title energy.
Q tail is visible at mobile size and gives the word proofs some motion.
8, 9, and slashed 0 are more distinguishable than the letter anchors, with counters doing some real identification work.
Support caps are readable enough in the phrase proof, especially A, E, X, T, V, W, and Z.
critique.verdict: Pass technically, reject as a design pass. The face has strong black mass, speed, and monster-title attitude, but the stated anchor reset is not visually proven. C/G/O/Q and 0/6/8/9 still share too much clipped-octagon construction, and the mobile proof collapses the supposed silhouette decisions into small internal nicks.
visualRisks: At small sizes, C, G, O, and 6 can be misread because the differentiators are too small and internal.
The repeated clipped-corner language may make the font feel derivative despite the strong title energy.
Q tails need protected spacing in real words; the raw proof is acceptable, but dense text could collide.
The black proof shows concept dependence on texture cuts rather than memorable letter silhouettes.
weakestProblems: C does not have the promised huge lower exterior jaw fang; it reads as a small bite or corner chip, especially on mobile.
G is still mostly C plus an internal notch. The inward voltage hook/bar is not bold enough to alter the counter or outer silhouette.
O uses an internal lightning bite more than a major asymmetric exterior charge break, so it shares the same ring logic as C/G/Q.
C/G/O/Q are a family of near-identical black octagonal chunks rather than four large silhouette-first anchors.
6 still looks too close to G/C construction; the numeral set is improved but not fully separated by counter architecture.
Generic esports chamfers remain dominant across support letters, weakening the promised creature-battle specificity.

critique.controlGroupVerdict: Fail. In the rows "C G O Q", "CGOQ AEX", "COCO", "GOGO", "QOQO", and "CCCC GGGG OOOO QQQQ", the control glyphs read as one octagonal module with minor edits, not four distinct anchor silhouettes.
critique.counterShapeVerdict: Weak. Counters are mostly faceted polygons of similar width and rhythm. The 8 has two counters, but 0/O, 6/G, and 9/Q do not yet have decisively different white-shape logic.
critique.glyphDifferentiationVerdict: Below approval. The alphabet is readable, but the required anchor differentiation is not visually proven. C needs a true exterior jaw, G needs a heavy internal bar, O needs one unmistakable outside charge event, and Q needs a longer tail with visible spacing protection.
critique.memoryLesson: See the anchor glyphs as black shapes first, not decorated letters. If C/G/O/Q still look like the same octagon after squinting at the mobile proof, the iteration has failed no matter how sharp the cuts look up close.
nextEdits: Redraw C as a wide open bite with one oversized lower exterior fang that changes the left and bottom silhouette before adding any interior cuts.
Redraw G from C, then add a thick inward bar/hook that visibly compresses or redirects the counter and alters the right edge.
Make O closed but give it one large exterior charge protrusion or bite that is not repeated on 0.
Give Q a longer comet tail with extra right sidebearing so it survives in "QOQO" and "Q9 QO QUICK".
Separate 0/6/8/9 by white-shape plans: 0 tall slash or offset counter, 6 lower-heavy loop, 8 unequal stacked counters, 9 top-heavy loop with a descending fang.
Reduce repeated clipped-corner chamfers across support glyphs so the anchor glyphs carry the identity instead of texture.
critique.promptPatch: Next generator pass: build C/G/O/Q as four black thumbnail silhouettes first. Ban small-nick differentiation during anchor construction. C = open jaw plus one oversized lower exterior fang; G = C plus thick inward voltage bar changing the right outline and counter; O = closed ring with one major asymmetric exterior charge event; Q = closed O plus long comet tail with protected right spacing. Only after these pass mobile silhouette sorting should support letters inherit the style.
proofStringFailures: "C G O Q": C, G, O, and Q share the same octagonal construction too closely.
"CGOQ AEX": the control group does not outperform the support letters; G and Q are especially under-authored.
"COCO": C and O differ mainly by an aperture, not by a jaw-fang silhouette.
"GOGO": G is too close to O plus a nick; the voltage hook is not mobile-visible.
"QOQO": Q tail is too small to separate the rhythm from O at a glance.
"OO00": zero and O remain near-clones in silhouette and counter proportion.
"O0O0 6G6G": 6 and G rely on small cuts; the counter architecture is not distinct enough.
"Q9 G6": Q/9 and G/6 remain confusable families instead of deliberate contrasts.
"0689 8906": numerals share the same clipped-corner texture too heavily.
critique.referenceStrategy: Use a two-column proof: left column filled exterior silhouettes with counters temporarily removed, right column final counters restored. Compare only C/G/O/Q and 0/6/8/9 before expanding the alphabet; reject any pair that remains distinguishable only by an inner chip, slash, or clipped corner.
critique.spacingVerdict: Serviceable but not design-proof. HOO/OHH and NNN/OOO are even enough, but "Q9 QO QUICK" exposes protected-tail spacing problems, and "Hamburgefontsiv" feels mechanically tight around GEFO and NTSI.
strongestDecisions: The reverse slant and heavy black mass give immediate arcade-title energy in HHH, NNN, AVATAR, and Toffee.
H, N, T, V, W, and Y are readable and carry the fast angular system without collapsing.
The support alphabet is calmer than prior noisy mutation directions; most letters are legible at mobile size.
critique.tastePracticeFocus: Perceptual skill: mobile silhouette sorting on the proof row "C G O Q"; pass condition: at thumbnail size, each glyph can be identified from its black exterior outline alone before looking at any interior nick or counter detail.
critique.verdict: Reject. The face has usable speed and title weight, but the key Voltage Fang approval gate still fails: C/G/O/Q and 0/6/8/9 are mostly cloned octagonal bodies differentiated by small bites, cuts, or tails. The metadata asks for silhouette-first anchors, but the proof still reads as generic italic esports bevels with minor monster damage.
visualRisks: The design can be mistaken for an off-the-shelf racing/esports italic display face because the bevel grammar repeats too uniformly.
Small interior nicks disappear first on mobile, especially in G, 6, 8, 9, and A.
C and G risk swapping identities in fast reading; O and 0 remain too close in OO00 and O0O0.
The Q tail may collide in tighter settings because it is treated as a small appendage rather than a spaced silhouette feature.
weakestProblems: C/G/O/Q are not large exterior silhouette events; they share the same octagonal ring DNA with only small openings, interior notches, or a modest Q tail.
0/6/8/9 still separate by clipped corners and small internal cuts rather than clear counter architecture.
O has no convincing major asymmetric exterior charge event; it remains a plain faceted closed ring.
G lacks a thick inward voltage hook that visibly changes the counter and right outline at mobile size.
C does not show a huge lower exterior jaw fang in the mobile proof; its lower feature is too small and too close to the generic chamfer language.
Q tail is present but too short and cramped to become a protected comet-tail identity.

critique.controlGroupVerdict: Fail, but closer. C/G/O/Q and 0/6/8/9 are visibly heavier and cleaner than the memory target, yet the group still relies on the same octagonal ring, same chamfers, and same right-side chip. C and Q pass individually; G, O, 0, 6, 8, and 9 do not pass as a differentiated system.
critique.counterShapeVerdict: The counters are readable, but too similar. O, 0, 6, 9, and parts of 8 use near-matching angular holes, so the white space does not explain the glyph identity. The 0 slash and 8 pinch need to be obvious white architecture, not incidental black texture.
critique.glyphDifferentiationVerdict: C is differentiated by openness and Q by tail length. G needs one unmistakable inward hook, O needs one major asymmetric exterior event that is not reused as the generic chip, 0 needs a stronger slash, 6 needs a true open top hook, 8 needs two clear counters, and 9 needs a descending fang tail that cannot be mistaken for Q.
critique.memoryLesson: This loop improved by making C and Q bigger silhouette decisions, but the eye still sees the O-like anchors as one repeated ring template. For the next iteration, ignore decoration and ask whether the white holes alone sort O, 0, 6, 8, and 9 at mobile size; if they do not, the font has not passed.
nextEdits: Make G from the successful C silhouette, then add one large inward lightning hook that occupies real counter space.
Redraw 0 with a thick diagonal slash that visibly crosses the counter in OO00 at mobile size.
Give 6 an open top entry and a lower-loop counter shape that is not copied from O.
Give 8 two distinct stacked counters with a pinched waist visible in 8O and B8/8B.
Give 9 a longer descending fang tail that exits below the baseline enough to separate it from Q.
Reduce the repeated right-side triangular chip on O-like glyphs so each anchor has one different silhouette event.
Tighten default sidebearings for O-like glyphs before relying on kern pairs.
critique.promptPatch: Next pass: raw black proof only. Preserve the heavy reverse-slanted title stance, but run a counter-sorting drill on OO00 and 0689 before alphabet expansion. Ban cloned octagonal counters across O/0/6/8/9. Make 0 use a mobile-visible slash, 6 an open top plus lower loop, 8 two stacked pinched counters, and 9 a descending fang tail. Keep C's open jaw and rebuild G from C with one large inward hook.
proofStringFailures: CGOQ AEX: G and O are too close; G's hook is not decisive enough at mobile size.
OO00: O and 0 are not separated enough; the zero slash disappears into the ring texture.
O0O0 6G6G: 0, 6, G, and O share the same octagonal-counter grammar.
Q9 G6: 9 does not carry a distinct descending fang tail and competes with Q/G logic.
0689 8906: numerals read as a set of related chips rather than four clearly engineered number silhouettes.
Hamburgefontsiv: A's white fang is noisy and the round letters open large spacing holes inside the word.
AVATAR Toffee: A repeats its small internal nick, while Toffee exposes loose spacing around O-shaped glyphs.
critique.referenceStrategy: Start with the mobile control rows only: CGOQ AEX, OO00, O0O0 6G6G, Q9 G6, and 0689 8906. Judge the white counter silhouettes first, then the black exterior shape, then review support words like Hamburgefontsiv and AVATAR.
critique.spacingVerdict: Conservative spacing keeps proofs readable, but O-like glyphs create visible gaps beside straighter letters. HOO/OHH, NON, AVATAR, Toffee, and YoYo show that the font cannot rely on pair kerning alone; the default sidebearings around round anchors need a better optical baseline.
strongestDecisions: The reverse slant, heavy weight, and compact caps give the specimen immediate creature-battle title energy.
C has finally moved toward a real open-jaw silhouette, especially in the CCCC row.
Q is distinguishable at desktop size because the tail is large enough to change the outer silhouette.
A, T, F, V, W, and Y support the aggressive display voice without needing color treatment.
critique.tastePracticeFocus: Perceptual skill: counter sorting in the OO00 and 0689 rows; target glyphs O/0/6/8/9; pass condition: at mobile proof size, each glyph can be identified from its white counter shape alone before reading the black outer contour.
critique.verdict: Reject for next loop. The face has stronger raw punch than the previous generic esports pass, and C/Q now show larger silhouette ideas, but the approval gate still does not pass on mobile: G, O, 0, 6, 8, and 9 are too dependent on shared octagonal counter logic and repeated right-side cuts.
visualRisks: Mobile users may confuse G/O/0/6 in short names or score-like settings.
The A internal fang becomes a small white noise mark at mobile size rather than a deliberate crossbar event.
The C is now dramatic enough that the calmer O/G/0 forms look under-designed beside it.
Dense rows of O-like glyphs create a stamped-mechanical texture, reducing the intended creature/voltage feel.
weakestProblems: O and 0 still share nearly the same octagonal counter and exterior rhythm; the slash in 0 is too subtle in OO00 and O0O0.
G is not a bold C-plus-hook; in CGOQ and 6G6G it reads as a slightly damaged O/C more than a distinct letter.
6, 8, and 9 do not have enough unique counter architecture. The 8 is especially weak because the two-counter idea is not clearly visible at mobile size.
The right-side triangular chip repeats across O, G, 0, 6, and 9, turning the key anchor group into a family of clones.
Spacing is loose and uneven before kerning in HOO/OHH, AVATAR, Toffee, and YoYo, with large holes around O-shaped glyphs.

critique.controlGroupVerdict: Partial pass. C/G/O/Q are readable, but only Q clearly changes the outside silhouette. C, G, and O still share too much clipped-octagon DNA, and G's hook is not bold enough to separate it from C or 6.
critique.counterShapeVerdict: Counters are clean and useful, but not distinctive enough. O, 0, 6, 8, and 9 all use similar hard polygonal bowls; 0's slash helps, while 8's center pinch and 9's tail need stronger counter logic.
critique.glyphDifferentiationVerdict: Improved but still below approval. 0 separates from O, and Q separates from O, but C/G/6 and O/8/9 remain too close in construction language at mobile proof size.
critique.memoryLesson: Do not mistake readable polygon bowls for successful anchor design. In Voltage Fang, approval starts when the white counters and outside silhouettes let you identify O, 0, 6, G, 8, 9, C, and Q in isolation at mobile size, without relying on repeated chamfers, nearby context, or the font's general attitude.
nextEdits: Redraw C with one large lower exterior fang that changes the silhouette more than any chamfer.
Make G inherit C's jaw but add a thick inward voltage hook that is visible at the mobile proof scale.
Break O with one major exterior asymmetry instead of distributing equal clipped corners around the ring.
Push 6 away from G by opening the top hook and enlarging the lower-loop counter difference.
Pinch 8's waist harder so the two counters have visibly different pressure and shape.
Give 9 a longer descending fang tail that is distinct from Q's comet tail.
critique.promptPatch: For the next generator pass, prioritize O0O0 6G6G as the approval row. Keep the heavy reverse-slanted black silhouette, but force four distinct counter systems: O is a closed ring with one large asymmetric exterior break; 0 is narrower with a bold slash and different side rhythm; 6 has an open top hook and dominant lower loop; G is a C jaw with a large inward voltage hook. Ban equal octagonal chamfers as the main construction move.
proofStringFailures: C G O Q: C is too generic, G hook is underpowered, O is a regular clipped ring.
0 6 8 9: 6 and 9 do not establish enough numeral-specific silhouette; 8 lacks a strong pinch.
CGOQ AEX: control glyphs look like one cloned system beside more expressive A/E/X shapes.
CCCC GGGG OOOO QQQQ: repetition reveals the sameness of chamfers and perimeter cuts.
O0O0 6G6G: 6 and G are too similar; O and 0 separate mostly because of slash, not outer rhythm.
Q9 G6: Q and 9 share loop-plus-tail logic, while G and 6 crowd each other conceptually.
0689 8906: numerals are readable, but 6/8/9 do not yet feel custom enough for the stated construction ideas.
critique.referenceStrategy: Use the current proof only as a weight and slant reference. Build a larger black-only anchor sheet for C/G/O/Q and 0/6/8/9, then inspect the mobile crop before expanding decoration or judging full words.
critique.spacingVerdict: Usable but uneven. HHH/OOO/HOO is stable, but HAMBURGEFONTSI tightens around BURGEF, AVATAR has uneven A/V/A rhythm, and Q9 QO QUICK exposes awkward gaps around Q/O pairs.
strongestDecisions: Heavy reverse slant gives the specimen immediate display energy without relying on color or effects.
Counters in HHH/OOO/HOO and nnn/ooo/non/ono stay open and readable in both desktop and mobile proofs.
Q has a visible tail in the C G O Q row, and 0 has a visible slash in the 0 6 8 9 and 0689 rows.
Uppercase word rows such as HAMBURGEFONTSI and AVATAR TOFFEE keep enough rhythm to function as title lettering.
critique.tastePracticeFocus: Perceive counter-family separation in the O0O0 6G6G row: at mobile size, O, 0, 6, and G must each be identifiable from outer silhouette plus counter architecture before reading the surrounding letters.
critique.verdict: Not approved yet. The face has better raw-black punch than the previous memory describes, and several anchors are now readable at mobile size, but the control groups still do not clear the Voltage Fang bar. C/G/O/Q and 0/6/8/9 are still too dependent on shared clipped-octagon grammar, with only the zero and Q showing strong enough identity from silhouette alone.
visualRisks: At mobile size, the repeated angled cuts flatten into generic esports texture.
The A damage notch is overused relative to its functional role and can feel like accidental dropout.
The family risks approval for attitude rather than for differentiated counter architecture.
Dense proof rows like CCCC GGGG OOOO QQQQ expose cloned construction more than style consistency.
weakestProblems: C still reads like a clipped hex C, not a creature jaw with a decisive lower exterior fang.
G is not far enough from C or 6; its inward hook is small and buried in the same angular cut language.
O remains a regular octagonal ring with cloned chamfers; the promised major asymmetric exterior break is not visually proven.
6 and G are too close in the O0O0 6G6G, Q9 G6, and 0689 rows.
8 has two counters, but the center pinch is weak; it reads as a conventional angular 8 rather than a voltage-specific construction.

critique.controlGroupVerdict: C/G/O/Q is improved but not approval-ready. C has a clear open jaw, O is a clipped ring, Q has a tail, but G is still a C with a small insert instead of a large inward voltage hook.
critique.counterShapeVerdict: Counters are the main failure. O, 0, 6, 8, and 9 do not yet have four or five distinct white-shape logics; most are variations on a hard octagonal aperture plus minor marks.
critique.glyphDifferentiationVerdict: Alphabet differentiation is stronger in A/E/X/V/W/Y than in the required anchors. The expressive support glyphs make the control glyphs look more cloned, not more resolved.
critique.memoryLesson: Look at the white holes first. If the counter shapes cannot name the glyphs at thumbnail size, added slashes, bites, tails, and chips are decoration rather than structure.
nextEdits: Redraw O as a wider closed ring with one large exterior asymmetry, not a regular octagon.
Make 0 narrower and give the slash a bold uninterrupted mobile-visible diagonal that changes the counter, not just the fill texture.
Rebuild 6 with an unmistakable open top hook and larger lower-loop dominance.
Redraw 8 with two stacked counters and a visible waist pinch from the white shape alone.
Make 9 use a longer descending fang tail that changes the outer silhouette before any internal detail is added.
Rebuild G from C with one oversized inward hook that occupies the mouth area and survives thumbnail scale.
critique.promptPatch: Next generator pass: run a black-proof-only counter sorting drill before alphabet expansion. Ban shared octagonal counters across O/0/6/G/8/9. Draw O wide and closed with one exterior asymmetry; 0 narrow with a dominant slash that reshapes the counter; 6 open at top with a heavy lower loop; G as C plus one large inward voltage hook; 8 as two pinched stacked counters; 9 with a descending fang tail visible in silhouette.
proofStringFailures: C G O Q: G hook is too weak; O and Q still share too much exterior logic.
0 6 8 9: zero slash, six opening, eight pinch, and nine fang tail are not distinct enough as a set.
CGOQ AEX: A/E/X have more character than the control glyphs, exposing the anchor weakness.
CCCC GGGG OOOO QQQQ: repetition reveals cloned chamfer construction.
OO00: O and zero are separated mostly by slash, not by counter architecture and width rhythm.
O0O0 6G6G: zero, six, and G are not reliably separable at mobile proof size.
critique.referenceStrategy: Use the existing A/E/X energy only as a sharpness reference, not as the construction model. Build the next proof around large black-and-white silhouette separations in OO00, O0O0 6G6G, C G O Q, and 0 6 8 9 before checking words.
critique.spacingVerdict: Spacing is serviceable in HHH/OOO and Hamburgefontsiv, but repeated control rows expose uneven rhythm. OO00, O0O0 6G6G, QOQO, and CCCC GGGG OOOO QQQQ need optical spacing judged before kerning.
strongestDecisions: Heavy reverse-slanted mass gives the face immediate arcade-title force.
A, V, W, X, Y, and some word rows carry sharper creature-battle energy than the control glyphs.
Mobile proof remains mostly readable at small size, with no styling effects hiding the raw drawing.
critique.tastePracticeFocus: Perceptual skill: counter-family sorting on the proof rows OO00 and O0O0 6G6G; pass condition: at mobile size, O, 0, 6, and G can be identified from the white counter shapes before reading the black exterior contour.
critique.verdict: A usable high-energy display proof, but the anchor drill only partially succeeded. The alphabet has punch and title stance, yet C/G/O/Q and 0/6/8/9 still read like related octagonal cuts with small add-ons instead of distinct silhouette systems.
visualRisks: At logo scale the font may feel derivative of generic angular esports lettering.
Small internal chips on A/0 and related glyphs risk becoming noise at mobile sizes.
Repeated equal chamfers flatten the intended comet-fang weirdness.
Spacing in dense rows can feel mechanical before kerning, especially repeated O/Q/G groups.
weakestProblems: O, zero, six, eight, and nine still share too much clipped-octagon DNA.
G is underpowered: the inward hook is too small to separate it from C at mobile size.
Zero slash is not consistently dominant enough; it becomes a texture mark rather than a numeral-defining structure.
Eight lacks a strong two-counter pinch and reads like a modified ring/loop system.
Nine tail is visible but not fang-like enough to separate it from Q by silhouette alone.

critique.controlGroupVerdict: Failing the approval gate. In "C G O Q", C is open but not jaw-like enough, G's hook is underpowered, O is a clipped ring with modest asymmetry, and Q's tail is the only clearly differentiated silhouette event. In "0 6 8 9", the numerals are legible, but their counter architecture still feels like one template with small edits.
critique.counterShapeVerdict: Counters are readable but not tasteful enough. The O/Q counters are clean, but 0/6/9 reuse the same angular aperture rhythm, and 8's double counter does not yet become a calm, unmistakable two-bowl structure. White shapes are present, but too many are small nicks instead of decisive forms.
critique.glyphDifferentiationVerdict: Moderate. H, A, V, W, Y, T, F, and S have clear identities, but the anchor set does not yet clear the silhouette test. C/G/O/Q and 0/6/8/9 must differ before texture, chamfer, or slant are counted as style.
critique.memoryLesson: The next loop should learn to distinguish identity from texture: if C, G, O, Q, 0, 6, 8, and 9 need tiny interior cuts to tell them apart, the design has not solved the font. Anchor glyphs must be recognizable as different black masses with different counter architectures before the shared Voltage Fang styling is applied.
nextEdits: Redraw C as an open jaw with one large lower exterior fang that visibly changes the outside contour at mobile size.
Make G's bar a bold silhouette event, entering from the right side and cutting into the counter enough to survive the "GOGO" and "6G6G" rows.
Give O one major outer asymmetry while keeping the counter calmer and rounder than C/G/Q.
Rebuild 0/6/8/9 from separate counter plans: slashed narrow 0, open-hook 6, calm two-counter 8, and top-loop 9 with a longer descending fang.
Normalize spacing before kerning using AVATAR, Toffee, CGOQ AEX, QOQO, and Q9 QO QUICK as the test strings.
critique.promptPatch: Next pass: keep the raw black reverse-slanted display weight, but rebuild the approval anchors from separate thumbnail silhouettes before propagating the alphabet. C = open lower-jaw exterior fang, G = C plus heavy right-side inward voltage bar, O = closed calmer ring with one large outer asymmetry, Q = O plus protected long comet tail. Numerals must not share one octagon: 0 narrow slashed counter, 6 open top hook and lower loop, 8 two clean stacked counters, 9 top loop with descending fang. Reduce small internal nicks and repeated clipped-corner decoration.

critique.referenceStrategy: Judge the next run only from the mobile black proof first. Cover the interior chips mentally and compare C/G/O/Q plus 0/6/8/9 as silhouettes; then check counters as white shapes; only after that look at logo words such as VOLTAGE FANG or HAMBURGEFONTSI.
critique.spacingVerdict: Usable but uneven. "HHH OOO HOO OHH" is serviceable, while "HAMBURGEFONTSI" runs cramped through GEFO and loose near SI. "AVATAR Toffee" has large gaps around V/A/T and uneven lowercase-style spacing in Toffee. "Q9 QO QUICK" exposes crowding around QO and a dark Q/U/I cluster.
strongestDecisions: Heavy reverse slant and dense weight give the face immediate arcade-title impact.
H, A, V, Y, T, and F read quickly and carry the high-energy stance without falling apart.
O and Q keep usable closed counters in the large proof, so the family is not just black texture.
The raw black proof exposes the real silhouette decisions clearly, with no decorative effects hiding weak glyphs.
critique.tastePracticeFocus: Perceptual skill: read anchor glyphs by outer silhouette first in the mobile rows "C G O Q" and "0 6 8 9"; pass condition: each glyph remains identifiable when its interior white bites are ignored except for the main counter architecture.
critique.verdict: Not approval-ready. The proof has strong velocity and a usable black-title mass, but the anchor glyphs still share too much clipped-octagon construction. The concept is visible, yet the mobile rows show that C/G/O/Q and 0/6/8/9 are not separated enough by large silhouette and counter logic.
visualRisks: At mobile size, C, G, O, Q, 0, 6, 8, and 9 compress into a family of similar black capsules with small white interruptions.
The face risks reading as a generic esports/racing font with a few monster-fang cuts rather than a creature-battle alphabet built from anchor logic.
Tiny internal nicks in A, G, 0, and 6 may fill in or shimmer in print, thumbnails, and low-resolution game UI.
Q's tail is distinctive in isolation but can over-darken adjacent spacing when followed by O, U, or I.
weakestProblems: C/G/O/Q still feel like variants of one clipped polygon shell; C lacks the promised unmistakable lower exterior fang in the visible row "C G O Q".
G's inward hook is too small relative to the outer shape, so in "GOGO" and "6G6G" it does not create a decisive silhouette difference from C or 6.
0/6/8/9 separation is only partial: 0, 6, and 9 repeat the same octagonal ring rhythm, and 8 is busy rather than structurally distinct.
The support alphabet repeats the same slanted chamfer grammar too often, especially in "HAMBURGEFONTSI" and "THE QUICK".
Spacing is uneven before kerning; "AVATAR", "Toffee", "CGOQ AEX", and "Q9 QO QUICK" show distracting loose and tight patches.

activeLearningTarget.tastePracticeFocus: Perceptual skill: read anchor glyphs by outer silhouette first in the mobile rows "C G O Q" and "0 6 8 9"; pass condition: each glyph remains identifiable when its interior white bites are ignored except for the main counter architecture.
activeLearningTarget.promptPatch: Next pass: keep the raw black reverse-slanted display weight, but rebuild the approval anchors from separate thumbnail silhouettes before propagating the alphabet. C = open lower-jaw exterior fang, G = C plus heavy right-side inward voltage bar, O = closed calmer ring with one large outer asymmetry, Q = O plus protected long comet tail. Numerals must not share one octagon: 0 narrow slashed counter, 6 open top hook and lower loop, 8 two clean stacked counters, 9 top loop with descending fang. Reduce small internal nicks and repeated clipped-corner decoration.

activeLearningTarget.memoryLesson: The next loop should learn to distinguish identity from texture: if C, G, O, Q, 0, 6, 8, and 9 need tiny interior cuts to tell them apart, the design has not solved the font. Anchor glyphs must be recognizable as different black masses with different counter architectures before the shared Voltage Fang styling is applied.

rubric.overallScore: 0-10, not an average; judge whether this feels worth shipping.
rubric.readabilityScore: 0-10, counters, spacing, letter identity, mobile clarity.
rubric.energyScore: 0-10, silhouette, rhythm, title-card impact, useful weirdness.
rubric.originalityScore: 0-10, learned from references without copying or bland mimicry.
rubric.compositionScore: 0-10, specimen layout, scale, hierarchy, browser proof quality.

tasteRubric.controlGroups: Judge H/O/n/o basics when present, then the project-specific anchors. For Voltage Fang the active anchors are C/G/O/Q and 0/6/8/9.
tasteRubric.counters: Treat inside white shapes as drawings. Penalize pasted-in bites, accidental holes, and counters that collapse on mobile.
tasteRubric.spacingBeforeKerning: Judge whether sidebearings create even word rhythm before pair kerning can hide problems.
tasteRubric.confusables: Check O/0, C/G, Q/9, B/8, I/1, S/5, and punctuation at desktop and mobile sizes.
tasteRubric.constructionLogic: Reward one coherent construction logic with glyph-specific jobs. Penalize repeating the same notch as the whole style.
tasteRubric.proofDiscipline: Raw black proof rows matter more than decorated hero specimens.
